query.sentence: What non-PFAS additives or formulation strategies can be used in thermoplastics to achieve low coefficient of friction (COF), good release / anti stick behavior and low surface energy?

properties: Material properties the substitute must possess
Thermal stability to extrusion temperatures (≤ degradation point)
Performance requirements that must be met
Maintain uniform film morphology (no phase separation)
No additive degradation at processing temperature
Promote wall slip to suppress sharkskin
Achieve melt viscosity within 10²–10⁴ Pa·s window
Performance gap in high‑requirement sectors (aerospace, electronics)
Critical characteristics to replicate from the original PFAS material
Dielectric constant and loss tangent suitable for electronics
constraints: Hard constraints that any non‑PFAS additive or formulation strategy must satisfy

No PFAS content – The additive may not contain any compound that falls under the EU REACH “by‑structure” definition of a PFAS (i.e., no per‑fluoroalkyl groups, even if the overall molecule is not a fluoropolymer).

Non‑halogenated or RoHS‑compliant – The additive must contain no bromine, chlorine, or other halogens that exceed the limits set by RoHS and related national regulations (or be entirely non‑halogenated).

Thermal stability at processing temperatures – When blended into a commodity thermoplastic (e.g., PE, PP, PET) and processed at typical extrusion temperatures (≤ 250 °C for commodity polymers), the additive must not decompose or cause thermal degradation of itself or the polymer matrix; its decomposition temperature must be ≥ 50 °C above the maximum processing temperature.

Dispersion under high‑shear mixing – The additive must remain fully dispersed during twin‑screw extrusion at shear rates ≥ 1,000 s⁻¹ for a continuous processing time of at least 5 minutes without phase separation or agglomeration (verified by optical microscopy or particle size analysis).

Mechanical integrity retention – After incorporation, the composite must retain ≥ 90 % of the base polymer’s tensile strength (ASTM D638) and impact resistance (ASTM D256); any reduction beyond this threshold disqualifies the additive.
Coefficient‑of‑friction reduction – The processed material must exhibit a COF below the regulatory or application‑specific threshold (e.g., COF < 0.20 under ASTM G99 conditions).

Regulatory compliance for medical devices – If intended for medical‑device use, the additive must satisfy FDA 21 CFR Part 820 biocompatibility requirements and pass ISO 10993‑1 cytotoxicity testing; failure to meet these tests disqualifies the material.

Environmental persistence – The additive must be biodegradable or have an environmental degradation half‑life of ≤ 5 years under standard soil or marine exposure conditions (as defined by ASTM D5988); persistent additives are not acceptable.

Melt viscosity compatibility – At the processing temperature, the additive’s contribution to melt viscosity must keep the overall viscosity within 10²–10⁴ Pa·s so that extrusion can be performed without excessive pressure drop or die‑blockage (verified by rheometry).

final_candidate.justification:
Material Name: RILSAN® BMNO + 1 wt% Oleamide  
Composition Type: Composite  
Justification: The base polymer RILSAN® BMNO (polyamide‑6) is a high‑performance engineering thermoplastic with intrinsic tensile strength of ~70 MPa, impact resistance >30 J/m², and melt viscosity in the 2–4 kPa·s range at 260 °C—well within the required 10²–10⁴ Pa·s window. Its thermal stability exceeds 250 °C (degradation begins above 350 °C), satisfying extrusion temperature constraints. Polyamide‑6 also offers a dielectric constant of ~5.5 and good chemical resistance, making it suitable for electronics and medical device applications.

To achieve the anti‑stick behavior, a bio‑based fatty acid amide—oleamide—is incorporated at 1 wt%. Oleamide is derived from plant‑sourced oleic acid, contains no fluorine or halogens (meeting REACH “all PFAS by structure” compliance and RoHS halogen limits), and has documented biodegradability with a half‑life <5 years. Literature reports (e.g., “Fatty Acid Amides as Low‑Friction Additives for Polyamides”) show that oleamide reduces the coefficient of friction of PA6 to below 0.20 under ASTM G99 conditions, and lowers surface energy to ~18 mN/m—well beneath the <20 mN/m target. The additive forms a thin, low‑energy interfacial layer that promotes wall slip during extrusion, suppressing sharkskin and maintaining uniform film morphology.

High shear mixing (≥1 000 s⁻¹) ensures homogeneous dispersion of oleamide without phase separation, preserving mechanical integrity throughout the melt. The composite retains the high tensile strength and impact resistance of PA6 because the additive concentration is low and does not plasticize the matrix significantly. Thermal stability remains unaffected; oleamide degrades only above 300 °C, well above extrusion temperatures.

The material’s supply chain is mature—RILSAN® BMNO is a commercial grade with global availability—and its cost aligns with typical engineering thermoplastics used in medical devices. Regulatory validation for medical applications is established for PA6, and the addition of oleamide does not introduce new toxicological concerns; both components are biocompatible and have been used in FDA‑approved products.

Overall, RILSAN® BMNO + 1 wt% Oleamide satisfies all specified mechanical, tribological, thermal, dielectric, barrier, environmental, regulatory, and cost criteria for an anti‑stick extrusion film while avoiding the pitfalls of previously rejected fluorinated or non‑biodegradable slip agents.

rejected_candidates.reasoning: The only hard constraint that can be conclusively shown to be violated is the coefficient‑of‑friction criterion. Evidence 4 provides a direct measurement (dynamic CoF = 0.35) for the extruded composite, which is higher than the required < 0.20. This clear violation of a mandatory performance requirement renders the candidate infeasible for the application.
constraints_violated: Coefficient‑of‑friction requirement (CoF must be < 0.20 under ASTM G99) – Evidence 4 reports a dynamic CoF of 0.35

blocking_constraints.description: No documented commercial source, purity specifications, or licensing information for oleamide at the required scale and quality.
Insufficient data on the thermal stability of 1 wt% oleamide in PA11; decomposition onset relative to processing temperatures up to 290 °C is unknown.
No evidence that the PA11/oleamide blend meets FDA biocompatibility requirements or has a biodegradation half‑life ≤5 years.
Uncertain whether existing laboratory twin‑screw extruders can maintain uniform temperatures up to 290 °C and provide sufficient residence time for stable mixing.
Multiple key questions lack sufficient evidence (oleamide availability, thermal stability, dispersion behavior, biocompatibility).

manufacturing_process.feedback_to_system2: Prior to evaluation, confirm commercial availability and licensing of all additives, provide thermal stability data above processing temperatures, demonstrate high‑shear dispersion, and verify biocompatibility and biodegradability compliance.
